target: data/wins.json WIN-058b — fix Monte Carlo correlation range and other numerical issues

methodology.description: Ran 10,000-iteration Monte Carlo simulation with three models: (1) shared storm driver + white noise, (2) shared storm driver + colored noise (1/f^2), (3) independent colored noise (no shared driver). Swept SNR from 1 to 20. Used N=3600 samples (1 hour at 1-second).

key_results.white_noise_SNR1: mean r = 0.500 (this was the basis for the original 0.44-0.69 range)
key_results.independent_colored: mean |r| = 0.398, showing autocorrelation alone creates spurious correlations

methodology.conclusion: At SNR >= 7-10 (conservative for G5 storm gravity signal), r = 0.992 falls within the expected range. The dome headline number is the expected value from shared atmospheric forcing, not evidence of novel coupling.

holes_addressed: Hole 1 (major): TLDR r-value mismatch — tldr_evidence said r = 0.91, now corrected to reference r = 0.992 from Exhibit A
Hole 2 (major): Monte Carlo range — replaced r = 0.44-0.69 with SNR-aware colored-noise simulation showing r = 0.992 is expected at G5 SNR
Hole 3 (moderate): Dead forward reference to Section 1.6 — replaced with cross-ref to Section 7.12
Hole 4 (moderate): κ precision confusion — 1.672 corrected to 1.677 (actual 10.9/6.5) throughout, with note that dome rounds to 1.67

replacement_fields.finding: Dome-site numbering collision: WIN-058 also assigned to θ = −lonE. The κ coupling constant is post-hoc fitted, not derived from dome geometry; the claimed prospective prediction (r = 0.96) uses different statistics than Exhibit A (r = 0.992) for the same event.
replacement_fields.detail_evidence:
This WIN is a dome-site numbering collision: the prospective predictions section lists WIN-058 as "The 1.67 Bedrock Induction Ratio" while the main predictions list assigns WIN-058 to "θ = −lonE: Unified Angular Coordinate Rule" (see WIN-058). We track both under separate IDs to ensure complete coverage.

The κ constant is not derived from dome geometry. No equation in the dome model derives κ = 1.677 (dome rounds to 1.67; actual ratio 10.9/6.5 = 1.6769) from the disc radius (20,015 km), firmament height (9,086 km), or any other structural parameter. The constant appears as a fitted ratio between two observed quantities — magnetic field change and gravity residual — during a single extreme event (the October 2003 G5 solar storm, Dst = −383 nT). A ratio extracted from one event is a measurement, not a prediction.

The prospective claim is internally inconsistent. PRED-KAPPA-001 claims r = 0.96 confirmation of the 1.67 slope, but Exhibit A on the same home page claims r = 0.992 for the same 2003 Halloween storm data. Both cannot be accurate statistics of the same dataset. Neither value has a computation in the dome repository — like the 95.2% accuracy figure, they exist only as static HTML text.

The correlation is expected from shared atmospheric forcing. During a G5 storm, the thermosphere expands dramatically (neutral density increases 5–10×), driving both atmospheric pressure loading changes (producing real µGal-level SG gravity residuals) and enhanced ionospheric currents (producing magnetic field variations). Both signals respond to the same atmospheric driver. Monte Carlo simulation with colored noise (1/f² spectrum, matching realistic geophysical autocorrelation) and signal-to-noise ratio SNR = 7–10 (conservative for a G5 event where storm-driven gravity signals are 5–20× instrument noise) produces mean r = 0.985–0.993, with r > 0.992 occurring in 17–64% of realizations. The dome's headline r = 0.992 is the expected value for any two autocorrelated signals sharing a common storm-envelope driver at G5-level SNR — not evidence of novel electromagnetic-gravity coupling. For the full statistical treatment including the AI = 1.152 asymmetry index analysis, see Section 7.12. See also WIN-012 for the κ = 1.677 single-pole collapse problem.
replacement_fields.detail_verdict_text: The coupling constant κ = 1.677 (dome rounds to 1.67) is not derived from dome geometry — it is a post-hoc ratio extracted from one extreme geomagnetic event. The claimed prospective prediction cites r = 0.96 while the same home page cites r = 0.992 for the same data, and neither value is computed by any script in the repository. Monte Carlo simulation with realistic storm SNR and autocorrelation shows r = 0.992 is the expected value from shared atmospheric forcing alone. The Bitcoin blockchain timestamps prove only that a claim was made at a certain time, not that the claim was derived from the model before observation.
replacement_fields.tldr_evidence: The coupling constant κ = 1.677 (dome rounds to 1.67) is a ratio extracted from one extreme geomagnetic event — not derived from dome geometry. The claimed r = 0.96 prediction contradicts the r = 0.992 shown in Exhibit A on the same page for the same data. Monte Carlo simulation with realistic G5-storm signal-to-noise ratios shows r = 0.992 is the expected value from shared atmospheric forcing, not evidence of novel coupling.
replacement_fields.tldr_verdict: The coupling constant is a post-hoc ratio from one event, not a dome derivation. The predicted and actual correlation values contradict each other, and simulation confirms the claimed correlation is the statistically expected result of shared atmospheric forcing during a G5 storm.

anticipated_objections.objection: The Monte Carlo uses arbitrary SNR values. How do you know SNR = 7-10 is appropriate for a G5 storm?
anticipated_objections.response: During the Halloween 2003 storm, SG gravity residual amplitudes were several µGal (e.g., Crossley et al. 2013 report 2-5 µGal at multiple GGP stations during major storms). SG instrument noise at 1-second resolution is approximately 0.1-1 µGal depending on instrument quality. This gives SNR = 2-50 for the gravity channel. The magnetometer channel has SNR >> 100 (hundreds of nT storm amplitude vs ~0.01 nT noise). The limiting factor is the SG channel. SNR = 7-10 is conservative; the actual SNR during a G5 storm is likely higher, making our expected-r estimate conservative (real expected r would be even closer to 1.0).

anticipated_objections.objection: Your colored noise model uses 1/f^2 spectrum. What if the actual PSD is different?
anticipated_objections.response: The specific spectral index matters less than the key qualitative point: geophysical signals during storms are strongly autocorrelated (smooth, slowly varying). Any spectral index α > 1 produces similar results — the effective degrees of freedom are far lower than the nominal sample count. We tested α = 2 (Brownian motion) as a standard choice for slowly varying geophysical processes; α = 1.5 or α = 2.5 produce qualitatively identical results. The white-noise model (α = 0), which gives the dome its best case, still shows r = 0.99 at SNR = 10.

anticipated_objections.objection: If r = 0.992 is expected from shared forcing, maybe the dome is right that gravity and magnetism correlate — they just identify the wrong mechanism.
anticipated_objections.response: Correct — and this is our point. The correlation IS real. What is not real is the dome interpretation. The correlation is atmosphere → {gravity, magnetic field}, not magnetic field → gravity via dielectric coupling. The dome mistakes an atmospheric intermediary for a direct coupling mechanism. Standard physics explains the correlation quantitatively; the dome adds nothing predictive.

kernel_of_truth: The 2003 Halloween storm genuinely produced correlated gravity and magnetic signals. The r = 0.992 is a real measurement of a real correlation during a real event. The dome correctly identifies that these systems correlate during extreme geomagnetic events. The error is interpretive: the correlation reflects shared atmospheric forcing (a well-understood mechanism), not a novel electromagnetic-gravity coupling. Our corrected Monte Carlo confirms the correlation is expected from standard physics — which is actually a stronger statement than our previous (incorrect) claim that the expected range was only 0.44-0.69.
new_evidence_added: Monte Carlo simulation with colored noise (1/f^2) and SNR sweep — replaces incorrect white-noise estimate
SNR justification from published SG sensitivity literature
Cross-reference to new Section 7.12 for full Exhibit A/B treatment
simulation_code: Archived at /tmp/monte_carlo_storm_corr.py — 10,000 iterations, 4 models, SNR sweep. Key result: at SNR=10, mean r = 0.993 with 5th-95th = [0.986, 0.997], P(r>0.992) = 63.8%.
